Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN and LEFT worked, but UP had no impact. Let me check the current position. The green moved DOWN then LEFT. I need to understand the movement pattern better. It seems like the green moves in a snake-like pattern where the tail follows behind. When the green moves LEFT, the tail also moves LEFT, so the green can't move back into the tail's space.
Let me check the current state and see if the box turned G.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Green/White:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W'):
        print(f"  {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print("\nBoxes:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('c', 'G') and n['pixels'] == 12:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green is at rows 28-31, cols 49-51. White tail at rows 28-31, cols 44-48. The box is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. The green is below the box (rows 28-31 vs 24-27) and to the right (cols 49-51 vs 44-47). The box is still c.
The green moved DOWN from rows 24-27 to rows 28-31 and LEFT from cols 53-55 to cols 49-51. The tail is now at rows 28-31, cols 44-48.
I'm going in circles here. Let me rethink the approach.
The key insight is that the green snake moves in a way that the tail follows behind. The green can't move into its own tail. To get adjacent to the box from the RIGHT, I need the green at rows 24-27, cols 48-50 (just right of the box at cols 44-47).
Currently the green is at rows 28-31, cols 49-51. The tail is at rows 28-31, cols 44-48. The tail extends to col 44, which is where the box starts. So the tail is overlapping with the box's column range.
I think the issue is that I need to approach the box from a different direction. Let me try moving the green UP to get to the box's row level, but first I need to move RIGHT to clear the tail from the path.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Instead of trying to get the green adjacent to the box from the RIGHT, let me try getting it adjacent from the LEFT, TOP, or BOTTOM. Maybe the box turns G from any direction, not just from the RIGHT.
Wait, earlier I observed that the box turned G when the green was adjacent from the RIGHT. Let me check if the box turns G from other directions too. Let me try moving the green UP to get adjacent to the box from BELOW.
The green is at rows 28-31, cols 49-51. The box is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. If I move UP, the green would go to rows 24-27, cols 49-51. That's above the box's row range... no wait, rows 24-27 is the box's row range. So if the green moves UP to rows 24-27, it would be at the same row level as the box.
But the green is at cols 49-51 and the box is at cols 44-47. So the green would be to the RIGHT of the box, at the same row level. That's exactly where I want it to be!
Let me try UP x 1 to move the green from rows 28-31 to rows 24-27.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green at rows 28-31, cols 49-51
# Box at rows 24-27, cols 44-47
# Try UP to get to same row level as box

action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
